data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-10T01:40:52.745Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically regarding synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a knowledge question about .NET migration changes. Let me answer based on what I know about the EF Core Cosmos DB provider evolution.

In EF Core 9 (/.NET 9), the Cosmos DB provider started throwing exceptions for synchronous LINQ operations by default, but provided an opt-in to allow sync-over-async behavior. In .NET 10, this opt-in was still available. The trajectory has been toward removing sync support entirely for the Cosmos provider since the underlying Azure Cosmos DB SDK is async-only.


By .NET 11, I'd expect the sync-over-async workaround to be completely removed, so any synchronous LINQ operations like `ToList()`, `First()`, or `Count()` would throw `InvalidOperationException` with no fallback option. The fix would be to convert all Cosmos DB queries to async patterns using `await` and async methods. So all the LINQ methods that previously had sync versions would need to be replaced with their async counterparts—`ToList()` becomes `ToListAsync()`, `First()` becomes `FirstAsync()`, and so on, including `SaveChanges()` becoming `SaveChangesAsync()`. This means any code using the Cosmos provider would need to be fully converted to async patterns.
